Farn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs LoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 6,2009
Phil Monroe
Council Member
City of Coronado
1825 Strand Way
Coronado, California 921 18
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-199
Dear Mr. Monroe:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") I and is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, we base our
advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of
other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government
Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May you participate in governmental decisions regarding litigation between the
City of Coronado and the Coronado Cays Homeowners Association when it is reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effects of a decision would result in an increase to your
homeowners' association dues?
' The PoliticalReform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
You have a conflict-of-interest in participating in the govemmental decisrons
because of your economic interest in your personal residence. However, it appears the
"public generally" exception will apply and, if so, you will not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision because of this economic interest. You also
have an economic interest in your personal finances, which will be reasonably
foreseeably financially affected by the governmental decisions. If the effect is material
($ZSO or more in a l2-month period), you will have a conflict of interest and may not
participate in the decision on this basis unless you can show that the public generally
exception applies, as discussed below.
FACTS
The City of Coronado (the "City") is made up, primarily, of two identifiable
areas. The historic area, known as the "Village," is the predominate residential, business,
and governmental center. The second area, developed in the 1970s, is located 4 miles
south of the Village, is connected to the Village by State Highway 75 (the Silver Strand
Highway), is nearly entirely residential, and was developed as the Coronado Cays (the
"Cays").
The Cays is a combination of residential homes and waterways, with
approximately 30 percent of the homes located adjacent to the waterways, providing
access to the San Diego Bay. The waterways contain boats and boat slips, some of which
are owned by the Coronado Cays Homeowners' Association (the "Association"). Others
are owned by private residential property owners. Most, if not all, residential lots on the
waterways have access to the water through boat slips, or docks and slips. Homeowners
throughout the Cays, however, have the ability to access docks and slips in the common
area through rental arrangements with the Association.
You own property in the Cays. Your property is located in Bahama Village,
which has residences adjacent to the waterways. Your property does not have direct
water access, but is located approximately 450 feet away from the nearest waterway.
You do not own a boat.
The design of the waterways contains a central channel for boat transit, an area of
boat docks and slips, and a section of "berm" that is next to the bottom edge of the
seawalls to provide support against wall movement away from the soils and into the
waterways. As part of its original development, the channels were dedicated to the City,
which is responsible for its maintenance. The retaining walls, also known as "seawalls,"
which form the sides of the waterways, are owned by and the responsibility of various
entities related to the Cays, which may include the Association in common areas, but
some are also owned by and the responsibility of private residential homeowners where
those retaining walls abut private residences.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 3
The Association and the City have a dispute regarding the responsibility for
maintaining the waterways. The City acknowledges it is responsible for the channels, but
claims this responsibility only includes the central channel of the waterway and not past
the boat/dock slips to the seawall, which includes the berms. The responsibility for
maintenance of these berms creates exposure to liability for damages if there is a berm
failure. The Association and the City have engaged in litigation, seeking a declaration
from the superior court defining each party's responsibilities.
lf the superior court rules in favor of the City, making the maintenance of the
berms the responsibility of the Association, you believe it is reasonably foreseeable that
each of the homeowners of the property located in the Cays will become financially
responsible for annual maintenance, periodic maintenance and repairs,liability insurance,
and liability for any uninsured, or underinsured, damages. It is estimated that property
owners in the Cays will be assessed each year for such responsibilities.
Due to the ongoing litigation, each of the property owners in the Cays has
contributed toward the Association's legal fees already, through annual homeowners'
fees. While no determination to apportion assessments has been made by the
Association's board of directors (depending on access rights, proximity to the channels,
or any other method), you believe that it is reasonably foreseeable that every property
owner in the Cays will be assessed some amount, although the exact amount is unknown
at this time.
To date, you have disqualified yourself from participating in any decisions related
to the City's litigation with the Association, as well as any work or studies performed by
the City related to the maintenance or improvements to the berms and channels.
However, you believe it is important for you to be able to represent your constituents in
the City, including those in the Cays, in decisions that will relate to these issues.
Therefore, you are now seeking formal advice to determine whether the facts presented
will allow your participation.
In our telephone conversation of December 10, 2008, you further informed us that
the City of Coronado has a population of approximately 26,000. There are approximately
1,250 properties in the Coronado Cays with a population of between 2,500 and 3,000.
Although you live in Coronado Cays, city councilmembers are elected at-large and do not
represent a specific district.
ANALYSN
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No.4
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the govemmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analyical framework. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)
SrBp ONr: Ans You A PuBLrc Orrrcrnl?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency" (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) Members of the
Coronado City Council are public officials within the meaning of the Act.2
SrBp Two: Wu,r, You nB M,lI<nlc, P.lntrcplrlNc IN MlxtNG, oR INFLUENCING A
GovnnxuENTAL DBcrsroN?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation L8702.L.)
A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or
she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation L8702.2)3 A public official is "influencing a
govemmental decision" if he or she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 872OO (*872OO filers" include city council
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for rhe
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars. absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
r Making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a public
otficial as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
tunction to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly owned real
property or business entity. (See Regulation 18702.4)


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 5
influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the City regarding the
decisions. (Regulation 187 02.3.)
Because you will be called upon to consider and vote on certain governmental
decisions regarding the litigation between the City and the Association, you will be
making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official position to influence a
govemmental decision.
Srnp Tsnrp: Wn.lr ARE YouR EcoNoprrc INrnRBsrs-Tur PossrBLE SouRCEs oF
Corurr,rcr oF INTEREST FoR You?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. 1(b));
An economic intefest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
o
a
a
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income
that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87 103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
You have indicated that you own your residence in the Coronado Cays, in which
we assume you have an interest of $2,000 or more. You have also indicated that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a financial effect on your personal
a


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No.6
finances in that you homeowner association dues may be increased. Accordingly, you
have an economic interest in real property and in your personal finances.a
Srrp Foun: Is Youn EcoNoprrc INrsREsr Drnrcrr,y lNvor,vnn rN THE
GovnnNuENTAL DncrstoN?
In order to determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of the
governmental decision on a given economic interest is material we must first determine if
your economic interest is directly involved in the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18704(a). For govemmental decision that affect real property interests - Regulation
18704.2 applies. For govemmental decision that affect personal finances - Regulation
18704.5 applies.
Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered
directly involved if the property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the
property that is the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)
The map you have provided indicates that your real property is within 500 feet of
the nearest section of waterways that is involved in the decision. [n our telephone
conversation you state that the distance was about 450 feet. Therefore, your economic
interest in real property is directly involved in the govemmental decision.
Personal Finances
Regulation 18704.5 states:
"A public official or his or her immediate family are (sic) deemed
to be directly involved in a governmental decision [that] has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family."
Accordingly, your economic interest in your personal finances is also directly
involved in the govemmental decision.
SrBp Ftvr: Wner Anr rnB Appr,rclnr,E MATERTALTTy STANDARDS?
Real Property
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
You have not identified any other potential economic interests. Therefbre, our analysis is limited
to your economic interest in your residence and in your personal finances through a possible increase in
your association dues.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 7
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the govemmental decision will have anyfinancial effect on the real
property." (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny ruIe." [n order to rebut the
presumption, one would have to establish that the decision would not even affect the
property's value by one cent. Because your have indicated that it reasonably foreseeable
that your homeowners association fees will be increased, we find that this would affect
the value of your property by at least a penny, and the materiality standard has, therefore,
been met.
Personal Finances
Regulation 18705.5(a) provides that a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
govemmental decision on a public official's personal finances is material if it is a least
$250 in arry l2-month period. (Regulation 18705.5.)
SrBp 6: RB.lsoNLsLY FoRESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision.
You have indicated that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a financial
effect on your economic interest in your personal finances, and because of this we have
determined that there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
real property economic interest. If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your
personal finances is $250 or more during a l2-month period, it too will be material.
Having determined that the governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material effect on your economic interest in your personal residence, you
have a conflict of interest and are precluded from participating in the decision unless an
exception applies. If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the govemmental
decision results in a increase to your association dues of $250 or more, you also have a
conflict of interest as a result of your economic interest in your personal finances and are
precluded from participating in the govemmental decision on that basis also. Of course,
as explained above, if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the governmental
decision on your personal finances is never $250 or more in any l2-month period, the


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 8
financial effect would notbe material and it would rro, preclude you from participating in
the governmental decision.
Srrp 7: Punlrc GnNrne,r,ly AND Lrcu,l,y Rneurnro PlRrtcpauoN
"Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic
interests is material, a public official does not have a disqualifying conflict
of interest in the governmental decision if the official can establish that the
govemmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests
in a manner [that] is indistinguishable from the manner in which the
decision will affect the public generally as set forth in 2 Cal. Code Regs.
Sections l87O7.l - 18707.9." (Regulation 18707.)s
We will first address the potential application of the "public generally" exception
to your economic interest in your real property. The general rule applying this exception
is contained in Regulation 18707.1. However, there are also several special rules that
pertain to certain specific situations. One of these is Regulation 18707.10, which is
limited in its application to a public official's personal residence, in small jurisdictions
only. [t provides, in pertinent part, as follows:
"The effect of a governmental decision on the residential real property
that is the domicile of a public official is not distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally if all of the following conditions are met:
"(l) The jurisdiction of the public official's agency has a population
of 30,000 or less and covers a geographic area of ten square miles or less;
"(2) The public official is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an at-large
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries
of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision;
"(5) The official's property is located on a lot not more than one-
quarter acre in size or not larger than 125 percent of the median residential lot
size for the jurisdiction; and
5 In December 2006, the Commission adopted Regulation 18707.10, a special public generally
exception that applies to an official's personal residence for smalljurisdictions. Unfortunately, Regulation
18707 was not amended to add the new regulation. Nevertheless, it would not make much sense if we
could not apply the new regulation until this oversight was corrected and, given that the Commission
intended the new regulation to be operative upon 30 days of its adoption, we will apply the regulation and
make the correction to Regulation 18707 later.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. 9
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the property that is the subject of
the governmental decision that are similar in value."
Based on the information you have provided: the property is your domicile;
Coronado has a population of 30,000 or less; you are elected at-large; your property is more
than 300 feet from the boundaries of the subject property; and, in viewing the map you have
provided, it appears that with the possible exception of a few properties at the southern tip of
the Cays, all properties in the Cays are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the subject
property. Therefore, if your property has a lot size of not more than one-quarter acre (or is
not larger than 125 percent of the median residential lot in the jurisdiction, and you are
required to live in the jurisdiction, and the geographic area of the jurisdiction is fewer than l0
square-miles, you meet the requirements of the public generally exception under Regulation
18707.10, and you are not precluded from participating in the governmental decisions
discussed herein on the basis of your real property economic interest.6
Finally, if you determine that you have a conflict of interest because the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the decision would increase your homeowners association dues
by $250 or more in a l2-month period, you may, nevertheless, not have a disqualifying
interest if you meet the requirements of the basic public generally exception under
Regulation 18707.1.
Under that Regulation, you must be able to meet a two-part test by showing that the
decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the same manner"
as it financially affects your economic interest. For decisions that affect a public official's
personal finances, "significant segment" is defined as lO-percent or more of the population of
Coronado.
The "significant segment" that would be affected by the governmental decision
would be the residents of the Coronado Cays, which you estimate to have a population of
between 2,500 and 3,000. You have indicated that the population of Coronado is
approximately 26,000. Therefore, if you can establish that the population of the Cays equals
or exceeds lO-percent of the overall population of Coronado, you will have met the first part
of this test.
Once you have established that a significant segment will be financially affected, you
must show that the significant segment is financially affected in substantially the same
manner as you. If the increase to homeowners association dues in the Cays is uniform, the
significant segment will be affected in substantially the same manner and the public generally
exception will apply. Accordingly, you will not be disqualified from participating in the
governmental decision despite the fact that the decision will have a material financial effect
on your personal finances.
u If you do not meet the other requirements, you may, alternatively, meet the requirements of the
basic public generally exception under Regulation 18707, as discussed herein in reference to economic
interest in your personal finances.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. l0
SrBp 8: Lrcer,r,y Rreumrn Plnrrcperlox
Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required
participation exception is applicable here, we do not analyze this exception.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: William
Senior
Legal Division

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. 1(b));
An economic intefest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
o
a
a
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income
that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87 103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
You have indicated that you own your residence in the Coronado Cays, in which
we assume you have an interest of $2,000 or more. You have also indicated that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a financial effect on your personal
a


File Nos. A-08-199
finances in that you homeowner association dues may be increased. Accordingly, you
have an economic interest in real property and in your personal finances.a
Srrp Foun: Is Youn EcoNoprrc INrsREsr Drnrcrr,y lNvor,vnn rN THE
GovnnNuENTAL DncrstoN?
In order to determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of the
governmental decision on a given economic interest is material we must first determine if
your economic interest is directly involved in the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18704(a). For govemmental decision that affect real property interests - Regulation
18704.2 applies. For govemmental decision that affect personal finances - Regulation
18704.5 applies.
Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered
directly involved if the property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the
property that is the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)
The map you have provided indicates that your real property is within 500 feet of
the nearest section of waterways that is involved in the decision. [n our telephone
conversation you state that the distance was about 450 feet. Therefore, your economic
interest in real property is directly involved in the govemmental decision.
Personal Finances
Regulation 18704.5 states:
"A public official or his or her immediate family are (sic) deemed
to be directly involved in a governmental decision [that] has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family."
Accordingly, your economic interest in your personal finances is also directly
involved in the govemmental decision.
SrBp Ftvr: Wner Anr rnB Appr,rclnr,E MATERTALTTy STANDARDS?
Real Property
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
You have not identified any other potential economic interests. Therefbre, our analysis is limited
to your economic interest in your residence and in your personal finances through a possible increase in
your association dues.


File Nos. A-08-199
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the govemmental decision will have anyfinancial effect on the real
property." (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny ruIe." [n order to rebut the
presumption, one would have to establish that the decision would not even affect the
property's value by one cent. Because your have indicated that it reasonably foreseeable
that your homeowners association fees will be increased, we find that this would affect
the value of your property by at least a penny, and the materiality standard has, therefore,
been met.
Personal Finances
Regulation 18705.5(a) provides that a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
govemmental decision on a public official's personal finances is material if it is a least
$250 in arry l2-month period. (Regulation 18705.5.)
SrBp 6: RB.lsoNLsLY FoRESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision.
You have indicated that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a financial
effect on your economic interest in your personal finances, and because of this we have
determined that there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
real property economic interest. If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your
personal finances is $250 or more during a l2-month period, it too will be material.
Having determined that the governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material effect on your economic interest in your personal residence, you
have a conflict of interest and are precluded from participating in the decision unless an
exception applies. If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the govemmental
decision results in a increase to your association dues of $250 or more, you also have a
conflict of interest as a result of your economic interest in your personal finances and are
precluded from participating in the govemmental decision on that basis also. Of course,
as explained above, if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the governmental
decision on your personal finances is never $250 or more in any l2-month period, the


File Nos. A-08-199
financial effect would notbe material and it would rro, preclude you from participating in
the governmental decision.
Srrp 7: Punlrc GnNrne,r,ly AND Lrcu,l,y Rneurnro PlRrtcpauoN
"Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic
interests is material, a public official does not have a disqualifying conflict
of interest in the governmental decision if the official can establish that the
govemmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests
in a manner [that] is indistinguishable from the manner in which the
decision will affect the public generally as set forth in 2 Cal. Code Regs.
Sections l87O7.l - 18707.9." (Regulation 18707.)s
We will first address the potential application of the "public generally" exception
to your economic interest in your real property. The general rule applying this exception
is contained in Regulation 18707.1. However, there are also several special rules that
pertain to certain specific situations. One of these is Regulation 18707.10, which is
limited in its application to a public official's personal residence, in small jurisdictions
only. [t provides, in pertinent part, as follows:
"The effect of a governmental decision on the residential real property
that is the domicile of a public official is not distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally if all of the following conditions are met:
"(l) The jurisdiction of the public official's agency has a population
of 30,000 or less and covers a geographic area of ten square miles or less;
"(2) The public official is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an at-large
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries
of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision;
"(5) The official's property is located on a lot not more than one-
quarter acre in size or not larger than 125 percent of the median residential lot
size for the jurisdiction; and
5 In December 2006, the Commission adopted Regulation 18707.10, a special public generally
exception that applies to an official's personal residence for smalljurisdictions. Unfortunately, Regulation
18707 was not amended to add the new regulation. Nevertheless, it would not make much sense if we
could not apply the new regulation until this oversight was corrected and, given that the Commission
intended the new regulation to be operative upon 30 days of its adoption, we will apply the regulation and
make the correction to Regulation 18707 later.


File Nos. A-08-199
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the property that is the subject of
the governmental decision that are similar in value."
Based on the information you have provided: the property is your domicile;
Coronado has a population of 30,000 or less; you are elected at-large; your property is more
than 300 feet from the boundaries of the subject property; and, in viewing the map you have
provided, it appears that with the possible exception of a few properties at the southern tip of
the Cays, all properties in the Cays are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the subject
property. Therefore, if your property has a lot size of not more than one-quarter acre (or is
not larger than 125 percent of the median residential lot in the jurisdiction, and you are
required to live in the jurisdiction, and the geographic area of the jurisdiction is fewer than l0
square-miles, you meet the requirements of the public generally exception under Regulation
18707.10, and you are not precluded from participating in the governmental decisions
discussed herein on the basis of your real property economic interest.6
Finally, if you determine that you have a conflict of interest because the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the decision would increase your homeowners association dues
by $250 or more in a l2-month period, you may, nevertheless, not have a disqualifying
interest if you meet the requirements of the basic public generally exception under
Regulation 18707.1.
Under that Regulation, you must be able to meet a two-part test by showing that the
decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the same manner"
as it financially affects your economic interest. For decisions that affect a public official's
personal finances, "significant segment" is defined as lO-percent or more of the population of
Coronado.
The "significant segment" that would be affected by the governmental decision
would be the residents of the Coronado Cays, which you estimate to have a population of
between 2,500 and 3,000. You have indicated that the population of Coronado is
approximately 26,000. Therefore, if you can establish that the population of the Cays equals
or exceeds lO-percent of the overall population of Coronado, you will have met the first part
of this test.
Once you have established that a significant segment will be financially affected, you
must show that the significant segment is financially affected in substantially the same
manner as you. If the increase to homeowners association dues in the Cays is uniform, the
significant segment will be affected in substantially the same manner and the public generally
exception will apply. Accordingly, you will not be disqualified from participating in the
governmental decision despite the fact that the decision will have a material financial effect
on your personal finances.
u If you do not meet the other requirements, you may, alternatively, meet the requirements of the
basic public generally exception under Regulation 18707, as discussed herein in reference to economic
interest in your personal finances.


File Nos. A-08-199
Page No. l0

Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required
participation exception is applicable here, we do not analyze this exception.

City of Coronado and the Coronado Cays Homeowners Association when it is reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effects of a decision would result in an increase to your
homeowners' association dues?
' The PoliticalReform Act is contained inFile Nos. A-08-199

CONCLUSION: You have a conflict-of-interest in participating in the govemmental decisrons
because of your economic interest in your personal residence. However, it appears the
"public generally" exception will apply and, if so, you will not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision because of this economic interest. You also
have an economic interest in your personal finances, which will be reasonably
foreseeably financially affected by the governmental decisions. If the effect is material
($ZSO or more in a l2-month period), you will have a conflict of interest and may not

exception applies, as discussed below.

CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886 January 6,2009 Phil Monroe Council Member City of Coronado 1825 Strand Way Coronado, California 921 18 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-08-199 Dear Mr. Monroe: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") I and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION May you participate in governmental decisions regarding litigation between the City of Coronado and the Coronado Cays Homeowners Association when it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of a decision would result in an increase to your homeowners' association dues? ' The PoliticalReform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File Nos. A-08-199 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION You have a conflict-of-interest in participating in the govemmental decisrons because of your economic interest in your personal residence. However, it appears the "public generally" exception will apply and, if so, you will not be prohibited from participating in the governmental decision because of this economic interest. You also have an economic interest in your personal finances, which will be reasonably foreseeably financially affected by the governmental decisions. If the effect is material ($ZSO or more in a l2-month period), you will have a conflict of interest and may not participate in the decision on this basis unless you can show that the public generally exception applies, as discussed below. FACTS The City of Coronado (the "City") is made up, primarily, of two identifiable areas. The historic area, known as the "Village," is the predominate residential, business, and governmental center. The second area, developed in the 1970s, is located 4 miles south of the Village, is connected to the Village by State Highway 75 (the Silver Strand Highway), is nearly entirely residential, and was developed as the Coronado Cays (the "Cays"). The Cays is a combination of residential homes and waterways, with approximately 30 percent of the homes located adjacent to the waterways, providing access to the San Diego Bay. The waterways contain boats and boat slips, some of which are owned by the Coronado Cays Homeowners' Association (the "Association"). Others are owned by private residential property owners. Most, if not all, residential lots
